mnemonics.title: Zebra Depth Layer
mnemonics.content: Think of Z-Buffer as a zebra with stripes representing different depth layers — closer stripes are darker, farther are lighter. Each pixel stores which 'stripe' (depth) it's on, so you only see the closest one.

mnemonics.title: Painter's Priority Palette
mnemonics.content: Painter's Algorithm: Paint like a house painter — start from the back wall (farthest), then paint walls in front, then windows, then doors. Same logic: sort polygons by depth, render back-to-front (P-A-I-N-T: Prioritize All Items Nearest — that's wrong, actually it's Back-to-Front).

mnemonics.title: Z-Buffer = Zero Confusion
mnemonics.content: Z-Buffer stores Z (depth) values. New pixel compares Z: if closer (smaller Z value), replace the old one. Remember: Z-Buffer keeps the SMALLEST Z (closest to viewer). Z = Zed = Zero = Smallest value wins!

mnemonics.title: A-Buffer = All You Can Store
mnemonics.content: A-Buffer (Accumulation Buffer) is like a running total — it accumulates coverage and transparency information for each pixel, handling antialiasing and transparency better than basic Z-Buffer.

acronyms.usage: Use when rendering scenes with many intersecting polygons. Stores depth value per pixel; closest surface wins. Remember: Smallest Z value = closest surface.
Use when you need transparency and anti-aliasing. Extends Z-Buffer with per-pixel fragment lists for accurate transparency handling.
Use for static scenes. Recursively divides space into two halves using a plane. Sorting done once for static geometry; faster real-time rendering.
General term for any algorithm that prevents drawing hidden surfaces. Z-Buffer and List Priority are both HSR techniques.
Remember: Painter's algorithm renders from farthest objects first, then closer ones paint over them. F-T-T-B = Far Things To Begin.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a grid of boxes. Each box has a number showing how far back it is (1 = very close, 100 = far away). When a new object wants to paint, check its number: if it's SMALLER than what's already there, paint over it. The smallest number always wins (closest to camera).

Imagine stacking clear plastic sheets. The furthest landscape is on the bottom sheet, closer trees on the next sheet, a house on another, and your character on the top sheet. You see through them if transparent, but each layer covers what's behind it. Stack from bottom (far) to top (near).

Think of students lined up by height for a photo — shortest (closest) at front, tallest (farthest) at back. List Priority algorithms sort polygons this way: create a queue sorted by average Z-depth, then render in order. Each new object knows who to stand behind.

Z-Buffer = check every pixel's depth (accurate but needs memory per pixel). Painter's = sort once, paint many (faster but fails with complex intersections). Picture Z-Buffer as a meticulous inspector checking every point, while Painter's is a lazy but smart painter who organizes brushes first.

Imagine cutting a cake with one knife cut, then cutting each half again, then again — each cut (plane) separates objects into 'front' and 'back' groups. When rendering, you always know which side of the cut the camera is on, so you draw the far side first.

analogies.analogy: Photography with depth: Imagine taking a photo and noting the distance to the nearest object in each pixel spot on your camera sensor. When a new object appears in that spot, if it's closer than what you recorded, update the photo. Like your eyes focus — you only 'see' what's closest.

Oil painting a landscape: First paint the sky (furthest), then mountains, then hills, then trees, then your house. Each layer covers what's behind it. If you painted the house first then tried to add trees in front, you'd have to repaint. Same with polygons — paint far ones first.

Organizing a queue for a school assembly: The class teacher makes a sorted list — shortest students at front (closest), tallest at back. Teachers then calls students one by one in that order to enter. The 'list' is sorted by 'priority' (height/z-depth) before any action begins.

Building a wall with bricks: You can only see the front face of bricks. Behind the wall, there are more bricks you don't see. HSR algorithms decide which bricks to actually 'draw' — saving paint (rendering time) by not drawing what gets covered anyway.

Think of Z-Buffer like a whiteboard with one square inch per pixel. Each square you record the distance to the nearest object. A 1920×1080 screen needs over 2 million recorded distances! That's why older games struggled — they needed this entire grid in memory for every frame.
